ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for chicken black olive fried rice; mapped to the broader 'fried rice food product' class as the closest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP covers fried rice as a category; no specific code exists for chicken black olive fried rice variant. Broader match used as the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for fried rice dishes; left null to avoid fabrication.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of chicken black olive fried rice as served in a Singapore food court or Thai restaurant, weighing approximately 450 g.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Fried Rice with Meat (SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite); supplemented with HPB Singapore Nutrient Composition of Food for local fried rice reference values
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service; Health Promotion Board, Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for chicken black olive fried rice exists in USDA FoodData Central or HPB Singapore database. Values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy composite for fried rice with meat (FDC ID 169957) and cross-referenced against HPB Singapore fried rice reference values. The input record's per_100g values (energy 160 kcal, protein 7.78 g, fat 6.22 g, carbohydrate 18.89 g) are consistent with this composite and are retained. Black olive contribution is minor at typical inclusion levels (~5–8% by weight) and does not materially alter the macro profile. Micronutrient values for calcium (11.11 mg), iron (0.67 mg), and vitamin C (1.11 mg) are plausible and consistent with USDA composite data for chicken fried rice. Sodium at 400 mg/100 g is consistent with restaurant-style fried rice using soy sauce, fish sauce, and oyster sauce.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; supplemented by University of Sydney GI Database entry for jasmine rice fried rice
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for chicken black olive fried rice. GI of 72 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for jasmine rice fried rice (GI ~72, glucose reference), which is the closest analogous food. Jasmine rice is the dominant carbohydrate contributor in this dish. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (72 × 84.99) / 100 ≈ 61.2, rounded to 60.9 using 18.89 g/100g × 450 g serving = 85.0 g carbohydrate per plate; GL = (72 × 85.0) / 100 = 61.2. Minor rounding applied.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 400 mg/100 g; a standard 450 g plate delivers approximately 1800 mg sodium, representing 78–90% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000–2300 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 150–200 g and request reduced soy sauce, fish sauce, and oyster sauce; black olives should also be reduced as they contribute additional sodium from brining.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI jasmine rice (GI ~72) with low dietary fibre (0.89 g/100 g) produces a high glycaemic load of approximately 61 per standard plate, driving rapid postprandial glucose elevation.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should request brown rice substitution to lower GI, limit portion to half a plate, and pair with non-starchy vegetables to increase fibre and attenuate the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium at 400 mg/100 g and saturated fat at 1.78 g/100 g are the primary cardiovascular concerns; however, black olives contribute monounsaturated fatty acids and polyphenols (oleuropein) which partially offset cardiovascular risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size, request reduced-sodium sauces, and note that the MUFA contribution from black olives is a modest positive factor, but the overall sodium load from a full plate remains clinically significant.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Black olives provide anti-inflammatory MUFAs and oleuropein polyphenols; however, sodium at 400 mg/100 g and refined jasmine rice with high glycaemic load may promote systemic inflammation.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis may benefit from the olive-derived MUFAs and polyphenols but should request brown rice, reduced-sodium sauces, and reduced brined olive quantity to minimise pro-inflammatory sodium and glycaemic load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken Black Olive Fried Rice (鸡肉黑橄榄炒饭) is a Thai-style stir-fried rice dish sold in Singapore food courts and casual Thai restaurants, notably under the '702D' brand. The dish is prepared by wok-frying cooked jasmine rice with diced or sliced chicken pieces, whole or halved black olives, egg, garlic, and a seasoning blend of soy sauce, oyster sauce, fish sauce, and white pepper. The wok hei (breath of the wok) technique imparts a characteristic smoky, slightly charred aroma. The flavour profile is savoury and umami-rich, with the black olives contributing a briny, slightly bitter, and fruity note that distinguishes this dish from standard chicken fried rice. The texture is fluffy and slightly oily, with tender chicken and firm olive pieces. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 160 kcal, 7.78 g protein, 6.22 g fat (1.78 g saturated), 18.89 g carbohydrate, 0.89 g dietary fibre, and 400 mg sodium. The high sodium content (approximately 1800 mg per standard 450 g plate) makes this dish a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The high-GI jasmine rice base (GI ~72) and low fibre content result in a high glycaemic load (~61 per plate), making portion control and brown rice substitution important for individuals with type 2 diabetes. Black olives contribute monounsaturated fatty acids and oleuropein polyphenols, which have modest anti-inflammatory properties relevant to rheumatoid arthritis management. This dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with soy, egg, fish, or gluten allergies without significant modification.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for chicken black olive fried rice exists in USDA FoodData Central or HPB Singapore database. Nutrient values are adapted from the USDA SR Legacy composite for fried rice with meat and cross-referenced with HPB Singapore fried rice reference values. The input record values are consistent with this composite and are retained. GI is estimated from the closest analogous food (jasmine rice fried rice) in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium due to the adapted source match and the absence of a direct laboratory analysis for this specific dish.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, B-vitamin panel, fat-soluble vitamins, and moisture data for this specific restaurant-prepared chicken black olive fried rice are not available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy composite or HPB Singapore database as of 2026-04-16. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.04 g/100 g from the input record, consistent with trace levels from cooking oil used in stir-frying; this is a plausible trace value and is retained. All core macros (protein_g 7.78, fat_g 6.22, carbohydrate_g 18.89) are non-zero and sourced from USDA FoodData Central adapted composite. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on jasmine rice fried rice from the University of Sydney GI Database. The dish is sold under the '702D' brand at Singapore food courts; the name '702D' refers to the stall identifier and is not a nutritional descriptor. Halal status is unconfirmed as fish sauce and oyster sauce brands vary; is_halal is set to false pending confirmation of halal-certified sauce brands used by the specific vendor.
